title: AAA Does Not Guarantee a Unique Triangle
description: Recognize that three angle measures determine only similarity, not a unique triangle.
condition: Given three angle measures (AAA) with no side lengths.
response: Conclude that the triangle is not guaranteed to be unique.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given angles $45^\circ$, $45^\circ$, and $90^\circ$, infinitely many similar triangles can be formed with different side lengths.

title: Add Areas of Composite Figure
description: Find the total area of a composite figure by summing the areas of its non-overlapping parts.
condition: A figure is decomposed into two or more non-overlapping shapes with known areas.
response: Add the areas of all component shapes to obtain the total area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a figure is split into a rectangle of area $24$ and a triangle of area $10$, then total area $= 24 + 10 = 34$.

title: Area by Composition or Decomposition
description: Find area of a composite figure by adding and/or subtracting areas of simpler shapes.
condition: Given a complex planar figure composed of rectangles, triangles, or other known shapes.
response: Add areas of included regions and subtract excluded regions.
label: Principle / Model
example: Total area $= 96 - 8 - 12 - 12 - 5.76 = 58.24$ square feet.

title: Area of a Circle Formula
description: Use the formula for the area of a circle in terms of its radius.
condition: Given the radius r of a circle.
response: Compute A = πr^2.
label: Principle / Model

title: Area of a Circle from Radius
description: Calculate the area of a circle given its radius using the formula A = πr^2.
condition: Given the radius r of a circle.
response: Compute A = πr^2.
label: Principle / Model
example: If r = 3.55, then $A = \pi (3.55)^2 \approx 3.14 \cdot 12.6025 \approx 39.57$.

title: Area of an Annulus by Subtraction
description: Find the area of a circular ring by subtracting the area of the inner circle from the area of the outer circle.
condition: Given an outer radius R and inner radius r of two concentric circles.
response: Compute πR^2 − πr^2.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Used in walkway brick area calculation.

title: Area of Composite Figure by Subtraction
description: Find the area of a complex figure by subtracting areas of non-included parts from a larger enclosing shape.
condition: Given a composite figure with known outer dimensions and identifiable subregions to exclude.
response: Compute area of outer shape and subtract areas of excluded regions.
label: Principle / Model
example: If outer rectangle is \(8 \times 12 = 96\) and excluded areas total 42.76, remaining area is \(96 - 42.76 = 53.24\).

title: Area of Regular Hexagon Prism Base
description: Find the area of a regular hexagon using the formula A = (1/2)ap where a is the apothem and p is the perimeter.
condition: Given a regular hexagon with apothem a and side length s.
response: Compute p = 6s, then A = (1/2)ap.
label: Principle / Model

title: Area of Trapezoid Base
description: Compute the area of a trapezoid using the average of the bases times the height.
condition: Given trapezoid bases $b_1, b_2$ and perpendicular height $h$.
response: Compute $A = \tfrac{1}{2}(b_1 + b_2)h$.
label: Principle

title: Area of Triangle Base
description: Compute the area of a triangle using one-half base times height.
condition: Given a triangle with base $b$ and perpendicular height $h$.
response: Compute $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$.
label: Principle
example: If $b=6$ and $h=3$, then $A = \tfrac{1}{2}(6)(3) = 9$ square units.

title: Area Scales with Square of Radius
description: Understand that when the radius of a circle is scaled by a factor k, the area is scaled by k^2.
condition: Given one circle’s radius is a known multiple of another’s.
response: Multiply the area by the square of the scale factor.
label: Principle / Model

title: Circumference Using Diameter
description: Find the circumference of a circle using its diameter.
condition: Given the diameter d of a circle.
response: Compute C = πd.
label: Principle / Model

title: Circumference Using Radius
description: Find the circumference of a circle using its radius.
condition: Given the radius r of a circle.
response: Compute C = 2πr.
label: Principle / Model

title: Construct Perpendicular Sides on Coordinate Grid
description: Create a right angle on a coordinate plane by drawing one horizontal and one vertical segment from a point.
condition: Given a starting point and requirement that the included angle is $90^\circ$.
response: Draw one horizontal and one vertical segment from the point to form a right angle.
label: Production / Skill
example: From $A(1,2)$, move 3 units right to $(4,2)$ and 4 units down to $(1,-2)$ to form a right angle at $A$.

title: Construct Rectangle from Given Vertex and Side Lengths
description: Given one vertex and the side lengths, determine coordinates of the remaining vertices by applying horizontal and vertical displacements.
condition: A rectangle with given width and length and one known vertex on a coordinate plane.
response: Add or subtract the given side lengths horizontally and vertically to generate the other three vertices.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $L=(4,4)$, width $=3$, length $=2$, possible vertices include $(7,4)$, $(7,2)$, and $(4,2)$.
notes: Assumes sides are parallel to coordinate axes as shown.

title: Construct Trapezoid from Given Dimensions
description: Construct a trapezoid on the coordinate plane using a given vertex, base lengths, and height.
condition: Given one vertex, trapezoid height, and two base lengths.
response: Plot the given point, construct one base of specified length, draw a parallel segment at given height for second base, and connect endpoints.
label: Principle / Model
example: From \((-3,5)\), draw a base of length 2, then 3 units vertically to form a parallel base of length 5, and connect endpoints.

title: Cross Section Parallel to Base of Prism
description: Identify that a cross section parallel to the base of a prism has the same shape as the base.
condition: Given a prism and a cross section parallel to its base.
response: State that the cross section has the same shape as the base.
label: Concept
example: For a triangular prism, a cross section parallel to the base is a triangle.

title: Cross Section Parallel to Pyramid Base
description: A plane parallel to the base of a pyramid produces a cross section similar to the base.
condition: Given a pyramid and a plane parallel to its base.
response: State that the cross section is a similar polygon to the base.
label: Principle
example: In a regular hexagonal pyramid, a plane parallel to the base forms a smaller hexagon similar to the base.

title: Cross Section Perpendicular to Base of Right Prism
description: Identify that a cross section perpendicular to the base of a right prism forms a rectangle.
condition: Given a right prism and a cross section perpendicular to its base.
response: State that the cross section is a rectangle.
label: Concept
example: For a right triangular prism, a perpendicular cross section is a rectangle.

title: Cross Section Perpendicular to Pyramid Base
description: A plane perpendicular to the base of a pyramid and passing through the apex forms a triangular cross section.
condition: Given a pyramid and a plane perpendicular to the base (typically through the apex).
response: Identify the cross section as a triangle.
label: Concept
example: In a right triangular pyramid, a vertical slice through the apex forms a triangle.

title: Diameter from Radius
description: Find the diameter of a circle by doubling the radius.
condition: Given the radius r of a circle.
response: Compute d = 2r.
label: Principle / Model

title: Exclude Specified Face from Surface Area
description: Subtract or omit the area of a specified face when calculating total surface area.
condition: A surface area problem specifies that one or more faces (e.g., floor) are not included.
response: Compute total surface area and subtract (or do not include) the area of the specified face(s).
label: Principle / Model
example: If total surface area is $8{,}000$ and floor area is $1{,}054$, then required area $= 8{,}000 - 1{,}054 = 6{,}946$.

title: Find Circle Area from Given Measure
description: Compute the area of a circle when given diameter, radius, circumference, or area, converting measures as needed.
condition: Given a circle’s diameter, radius, circumference, or area and asked to compare or determine area.
response: Use A = \pi r^2, converting diameter to radius (r = d/2) or circumference to radius (r = C/(2\pi)) as needed.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a circle has circumference 18.84 m and \(\pi = 3.14\), then \(r = \frac{18.84}{2\cdot3.14} = 3\) and \(A = 3.14 \cdot 3^2 = 28.26\).

title: Identify Impossible Pyramid Cross Section
description: Determine that certain polygons (e.g., square in a triangular pyramid) cannot result from specified plane orientations.
condition: Given a triangular pyramid and a specified plane orientation (parallel, perpendicular, or oblique to base).
response: State that a specified polygon (e.g., square) cannot be formed as a cross section.
label: Principle
example: A plane cutting a triangular pyramid cannot form a square cross section because the pyramid has only triangular faces.

title: Identify Vertical Angles in a Diagram
description: Recognize a pair of vertical angles as opposite angles formed by two intersecting lines.
condition: A diagram of two intersecting lines forming four angles.
response: Identify the pair of opposite (non-adjacent) angles as vertical angles.
label: Category
example: When two lines intersect in an X shape, the angles across from each other are vertical angles.

title: Lateral Area of a Prism
description: Compute the lateral surface area of a prism as the sum of the areas of its rectangular side faces.
condition: Given a prism with known base perimeter $P$ and height $h$.
response: Compute lateral area as $P \times h$.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $P=20$ and $h=10$, lateral area $= 20 \times 10 = 200$.

title: Match Cross Section to Given Diagram
description: Match a determined cross-sectional shape to a labeled answer choice.
condition: Given a determined cross-sectional polygon and multiple labeled diagram choices.
response: Select the letter corresponding to the matching diagram.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the cross section is a hexagon with all sides 2 cm, select choice F.

title: Radius from Circumference
description: Solve for the radius of a circle using the formula C = 2πr.
condition: Given circumference C and value of π.
response: Compute r = C / (2π).
label: Principle / Model

title: Round to Nearest Whole Number
description: Round a decimal to the nearest whole number.
condition: Given a decimal value and instruction to round to nearest whole number.
response: If decimal part ≥ 0.5, round up; otherwise round down.
label: Rule / Plan
example: $7.6 \approx 8$.

title: SAS Congruence Guarantees Uniqueness
description: Recognize that two side lengths and their included angle determine exactly one unique triangle.
condition: Given two side lengths and the included angle measure (SAS).
response: Conclude exactly one unique triangle can be constructed.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given sides 4 in and 7 in with included angle $35^\circ$, exactly one triangle can be formed.

title: SSA Does Not Guarantee Uniqueness
description: Recognize that two side lengths and a non-included angle may produce zero, one, or two triangles.
condition: Given two side lengths and a non-included angle (SSA).
response: Conclude a unique triangle is not guaranteed.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given sides 14 cm and 16 cm with a non-included angle of $30^\circ$, more than one triangle may be possible.

title: SSS Congruence Guarantees Uniqueness
description: Recognize that three side lengths determine exactly one unique triangle (if triangle inequality holds).
condition: Given three side lengths that satisfy the triangle inequality.
response: Conclude exactly one unique triangle can be constructed.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given side lengths 7 cm, 9 cm, and 12 cm, exactly one triangle can be formed.

title: Substitute 3.14 for π
description: Replace π with 3.14 when instructed to use this approximation.
condition: Problem states to use 3.14 for π.
response: Substitute 3.14 in place of π in calculations.
label: Fact

title: Surface Area of a Rectangular Prism
description: Compute the total surface area of a rectangular prism by summing areas of all six faces.
condition: Given a rectangular prism with dimensions $l$, $w$, and $h$.
response: Compute $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$.
label: Principle / Model
notes: In Q3, interior faces between stacked prisms are excluded.

title: Surface Area of a Rectangular Pyramid
description: Compute the surface area of a rectangular pyramid as the sum of the base area and the areas of its four triangular faces.
condition: Given base dimensions and slant heights of a right rectangular pyramid.
response: Compute base area and add areas of all triangular faces.
label: Principle / Model
example: If base is $6 \times 6$ and each triangular face has area $21$, total surface area $= 36 + 4(21) = 120$.

title: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem
description: The interior angles of a triangle sum to 180 degrees.
condition: A triangle with two known interior angle measures.
response: Compute the third angle as $180^\circ$ minus the sum of the two given angles.
label: Principle / Model
example: If angles are $45^\circ$ and $60^\circ$, third angle is $180 - (45 + 60) = 75^\circ$.

title: Triangle Inequality Theorem
description: A triangle can be formed only if the sum of any two side lengths is greater than the third side length.
condition: Three proposed side lengths of a triangle.
response: Verify that each pairwise sum of sides is strictly greater than the remaining side.
label: Principle / Model
example: For sides $9, 11, 13$: $9+11>13$, $9+13>11$, $11+13>9$, so exactly one triangle is possible.
notes: Equality (sum equals third side) does not form a triangle. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Two Angles Must Sum to Less Than 180°
description: To determine if a triangle can be formed from two given angle measures, verify that their sum is less than 180°.
condition: Given two angle measures of a triangle.
response: Add the two measures and check that their sum is less than 180°.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given $86^\circ$ and $94^\circ$: $86 + 94 = 180$, so no triangle is possible.
notes: If sum equals or exceeds 180°, a triangle cannot be formed.

title: Volume of Composite Solid by Addition
description: Find the total volume of a composite solid by summing the volumes of its component solids.
condition: Given a solid composed of multiple prisms or pyramids with known dimensions.
response: Compute each component volume and add them to find total volume.
label: Principle
example: If a solid consists of two rectangular prisms (each $V= lwh$) and one triangular prism ($V=Bh$), then $V_{total}=V_1+V_2+V_3$.

title: Volume of Right Prism
description: Calculate the volume of a right prism as the area of its base times its height.
condition: Given a right prism with known base area and height (distance between bases).
response: Compute $V = B h$ where $B$ is area of base.
label: Principle
example: If base area $B=18$ and height $h=4$, then $V = 18 \times 4 = 72$ cubic units.
